Ahh I think I see what the problem is. $PYTHONPATH is just supposed to point to the current directory, it shouldn't be that whole string. The reasoning is that you want your test files to search in your current directory first when they do ``import rtv``, instead of loading it from your site packages or wherever you installed rtv with pip.

```bash
$ export PYTHONPATH=.
$ echo $PYTHONPATH
.
$ py.test --record-mode=once --refresh-token=~/refresh-token
================================ test session starts ================================
platform linux -- Python 3.4.0, pytest-2.9.1, py-1.4.31, pluggy-0.3.1
rootdir: /home/michael/Projects/rtv_project, inifile: 
plugins: xdist-1.14, cov-2.2.0
collected 113 items 

tests/test_config.py ......
```

`https://github.com/jayvdb` is an invalid github remote url.
`https://gitlab.com/jayvdb` is an invalid gitlab remote url.
etc.

that is because we *know* those sites host repos under user/org/group prefix.

but the git world is not only github & gitlab.  While a library may give special treatment to very common cases, it should not do so at the expense of other git hosting.

`https://example.com/jayvdb` is assumed to be a git remote url for a repo called `jayvdb.git` hosted by `example.com`.  that is the only possible way to parse it, and not parsing it that way means that valid URL would be rejected, which would be a bug.

https://github.com/apache/incubator-airflow/pull/3572#discussion-diff-200991459R40

On that configuration transfer as far as I understand the only thing a task really needs are the connections it uses (conn_ids). In https://github.com/apache/incubator-airflow/pull/1391 I played around with grabbing that information from an API - worked pretty well. This is nice because it allows you to fine grain access to those connections. This issue to solve then is how to make sure that someone running "airflow run" xxx cannot access those connection ids. This requires some key passing from the scheduler to task.

This can also be solved (partially) by passing the configuration. However to do that properly and only pass the information the task needs you would need to find out what connections the task requires. On the other hand passing it at run time by the scheduler already solves one issue (you cannot do airflow run xxx as you dont have the config).

I think an issue could lie in backfills and subdagoperators you would need to pass the config here as well, if you want to do that sanitized it might be easier to do it with a key and have API access.

It wont solve the logging issue except when you pass in that config that it should log to a facility other than a file it does not have access to.

In that case it would be safer to use a proper sentinel instead of an empty dict:

```python
default_sentinel = object()
obj = Variable.get(key, default_var=default_sentinel)  # deserialize_json=False is the default, right?
if obj is default_sentinel:
    ...
```

Though, at this point, maybe it's better to ask forgiveness rather than permission:

```python
try:
    obj = Variable.get(key, deserialize_json=deserialize_json)
except ValueError:
    Variable.set(key, default_var, serialize_json=deserialize_json)
    return default_var
```

(as an aside, looking at this says to me that `Variable.get` should be raising a `KeyError` instead of a `ValueError`)

So here is the parse method for DbExportCommand:

def parse(cls, args):
        """       Parse command line arguments to construct a
dictionary of command       parameters that can be used to create a
command       Args:           `args`: sequence of arguments
Returns:           Dictionary that can be used in create method
Raises:           ParseError: when the arguments are not correct
"""

        try:
            (options, args) = cls.optparser.parse_args(args)
            if options.mode not in ["1", "2"]:
                raise ParseError("mode must be either '1' or '2'",
                                 cls.optparser.format_help())

            if (options.dbtap_id is None) or (options.db_table is None):
                raise ParseError("dbtap_id and db_table are required",
                                 cls.optparser.format_help())

            if options.mode is "1":
                if options.hive_table is None:
                    raise ParseError("hive_table is required for mode 1",
                                     cls.optparser.format_help())
            elif options.export_dir is None:    # mode 2
                raise ParseError("export_dir is required for mode 2",
                                 cls.optparser.format_help())

            if options.db_update_mode is not None:
                if options.db_update_mode not in ["allowinsert", "updateonly"]:
                    raise ParseError("db_update_mode should either be
left blank for append "
                                     "mode or be 'updateonly' or 'allowinsert'",
                                     cls.optparser.format_help())
                if options.db_update_mode is "updateonly":
                    if options.db_update_keys is None:
                        raise ParseError("db_update_keys is required
when db_update_mode "
                                         "is 'updateonly'",
                                         cls.optparser.format_help())
                elif options.db_update_keys is not None:
                    raise ParseError("db_update_keys is used only when
db_update_mode "
                                     "is 'updateonly'",
                                     cls.optparser.format_help())

        except OptionParsingError as e:
            raise ParseError(e.msg, cls.optparser.format_help())
        except OptionParsingExit as e:
            return None

        v = vars(options)
        v["command_type"] = "DbExportCommand"
        return v


And here is the parse method for DbImportCommand:

def parse(cls, args):
        """        Parse command line arguments to construct a
dictionary of command        parameters that can be used to create a
command        Args:            `args`: sequence of arguments
Returns:            Dictionary that can be used in create method
 Raises:            ParseError: when the arguments are not correct
   """

        try:
            (options, args) = cls.optparser.parse_args(args)
            if options.mode not in ["1", "2"]:
                raise ParseError("mode must be either '1' or '2'",
                                 cls.optparser.format_help())

            if (options.dbtap_id is None) or (options.db_table is None):
                raise ParseError("dbtap_id and db_table are required",
                                 cls.optparser.format_help())

            # TODO: Semantic checks for parameters in mode 1 and 2

        except OptionParsingError as e:
            raise ParseError(e.msg, cls.optparser.format_help())
        except OptionParsingExit as e:
            return None

        v = vars(options)
        v["command_type"] = "DbImportCommand"
        return v

>In fact, we could add lambda and diam to OpticalPSF (and maybe even deprecate lam_over_diam which is a bit unwieldy, imo)

I always thought the `lam_over_diam` was a bit weird too, since the natural thing to do is to divide the wavelength by the diameter in consistent length units.  This usually does the wrong thing, of course, since (I assume) most users specify `scale` in arcseconds, or maybe pixels, but not radians.  Having separate `lam` (can't use `lambda`, that's a python reserved word) and `diam` keywords probably makes this even more confusing though, since you would need to specify the wavelength in something like "meters arcseconds" and the diameter in "meters".  We could add a third keyword like `radians_in_one_scale_unit`, I suppose, but then we're starting to make the API unwieldy again.  I like the idea of a unified API, but I'm not sure if it's worth replacing one required argument with three for the non-chromatic `OpticalPSF`.

I see a few problems with this approach for backward and forward compatibility:

1) .pth files can contain comments, which aren't handled here
2) .pth files can contain relative paths, which wouldn't make sense to directly yield
3) .pth files can contain paths that don't exist, which isn't checked here
4) technically, .pth files that begin with `import` can contain executable code, which isn't handled here (and probably should not be - but at least should detect and avoid emitting raw code)

so, if we must rely on our own .pth file parsing it'd probably be good to extract a separate, general purpose .pth file parser function into `pex.util` and test that separately.

you can probably lift much of the required logic directly from the `site` module's .pth handling implementation here: https://github.com/kwlzn/python-sources/blob/master/Python-2.7.12/Lib/site.py#L131

It seems like this logic is doing two different things: (1) it is getting numbers like "Flights from 2 airlines", or something; (2) it is getting times like "flights around 7", without an "am" or "pm".  The trouble is it's not doing (2) correctly, because "flights at 7" will map to "7", not "700".  It looks like you're also double counting "7" and "7am" here, as this one will also match "7am".

So, I'd recommend treating the first case here, and you can do that with something much simpler than a regex: just iterate over the tokens in the utterance, and try `int(token.text)`, keeping the value if it succeeds.  Then leave the time matching to `get_times_from_utterance`, being sure to have this logic not match things like "7am".

Maybe I'm missing something, but `thread stop` will cause the device to detach from the network and can then manage its Delay Timer based on its local pending dataset.  My previous point is that we should restart the delay timer when:

1. the device has attached to a network and received a Pending Dataset from its Parent.
2. the device has detached from the network on its own (i.e. due to loss of connectivity).

I understand that we currently need to stop the Delay Timer as part of `Mle::Stop()` and that is fine for now.  `Mle::Stop()` is only called as part of `thread stop`, so it's fine to stop the Delay Timer in there for now.  But we should also consider restarting the Delay Timer if the device has detached from the network on its own (i.e. due to losing connectivity with its Parent).  Remember that there are two different reasons for detaching from the network: (i) the device detaches due to calling `thread stop` and (ii) the device detaches due to loss of connectivity.  Right now, you are attempting to handle (i).  I think we can also handle (ii) properly.

This is a case that #61 didn't cover, and exists for backward compatibility with the first release of tox-travis. With that first release, you would declare what environments got run for each version of Python. It didn't look at the environments that were listed at all, it just sent the string in the configuration verbatim, through the TOXENV environment variable. If the specified environment is not declared in the tox config, Tox will still happily run that environment, doing the best it can based on the settings it has.

When we switched to matching based on factors, so that by default on Python 3.4 all envs with `py34` in the name would run, that obviously was inherently in conflict with the original mechanism. However, I was able to maintain partial backward compatibility by saying that if there was only one "factor" being matched (`python`, which is automatic), and no environments matched it, then whatever the setting is will be used verbatim, in the same way as was done in the original version.

I would prefer that we simply snap to CS conventions and leverage that intuition.

Operators are declared - their signature and semantics are specified, but their implementation is opaque.
Graphs are defined - their signature and semantics are specified, and their implementation is transparent.

This is consistent with other language specs. In C:
     extern float add(float a, float b);              // declares add as a name that can be referenced
     float add(float a, float b) { return a + b; } // defines the implementation of add 

I prefer declaration, but am fine with specification or contract. Definition implies a specific implementation, which I'm not a fan of.

Regarding @NiklasGustafsson 's question as to who has the option: I believe it is a contract between both the caller and the operator, which unfortunately is only informally captured in the operator description. Since this part is not formally captured, "model validation" cannot check these aspects of the contract (e.g., such as "an output is computed iff a specific attribute has a specific value"). This, in turn, means that the semantics of what happens during a model's execution if this contract is violated is undefined.
